name: Bestla
description: Bestla is a jötunn (giantess) in Norse mythology, the daughter of Bölþorn. She is primarily known as the mother of Odin, Vili, and Vé, the first generation of Æsir gods, making her a crucial figure in the Norse pantheon's lineage. Bestla's union with Borr, son of Búri, resulted in the birth of these powerful gods who shaped the cosmos and established order. While specific details about her personality and deeds are scarce in surviving Norse texts, her significance lies in her role as a progenitor of the Æsir, linking them to the older race of giants. This connection highlights the complex relationship between gods and giants in Norse cosmology, where both forces are intertwined in the creation and destiny of the world. Bestla's influence extends through her sons, who are credited with slaying the giant Ymir and using his body to create the world. Her existence underscores the cyclical nature of creation and destruction inherent in Norse beliefs, representing a vital link between the primordial chaos of the giants and the structured world governed by the gods. She represents the power of feminine influence and the foundational role of mothers in the creation of powerful lineages. While not actively worshipped, she is revered as an important ancestress of the Æsir.
subtitle: Mother of Odin and Ancestress of Gods

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Odin
keyMyths.description: Bestla, daughter of Bölþorn, gives birth to Odin, Vili, and Vé with Borr. These sons later become the rulers of Asgard and the creators of the world.

keyMyths.title: Bestla's Lineage
keyMyths.description: As a giantess, Bestla's origin connects the Æsir gods to the race of giants. This highlights the intertwined relationship between order and chaos in Norse cosmology.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of the World
keyMyths.description: Bestla's sons, Odin, Vili, and Vé, slay Ymir and use his body to create the world. This act establishes the reign of the gods and brings order to the cosmos.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
